title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two given nodes in the tree. According to the definition of LCA on a binary tree, the lowest common ancestor is defined between two nodes p and q as the deepest node that is an ancestor of both p and q. An ancestor of a node is any predecessor node on the path from the root to that node.

examples.explanation: 3 is the ancestor of both 5 and 1. It is the lowest such ancestor.
5 is the ancestor of 4 because 4 is a child of 2, which is a child of 5. Thus, 5 is the lowest common ancestor.
1 is the root and the ancestor of both 2 and 3, making it their lowest common ancestor.

constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^5].
Both p and q exist in the tree.
hints: Consider using a recursive approach to traverse the tree.
Keep track of whether you've found either p or q as you traverse.
If you find p and q in different subtrees, the current node is the LCA.
Try to visualize the tree to understand the relationships between nodes.
